title: Housewarming

jokes.text: He told me he doesn't want my help, so I'm just gonna play the supportive partner, and watch him fail.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks David what time Patrick's housewarming is, implying he would know as Patrick's boyfriend.
jokes.punchline: David reveals he's been frozen out of party planning and has pivoted to passive-aggressively rooting for failure while performing supportiveness.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential David Rose — the simultaneous performance of good behavior and gleeful anticipation of disaster. The line is efficient, character-specific, and immediately establishes the A-plot tension with a laugh. The juxtaposition of 'supportive partner' and 'watch him fail' in one breath is the structure doing real work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to be there for Patrick. I'm going to support him through this whole thing. And by support, I mean I'm going to watch him fail spectacularly, and then I'm going to make sure everyone knows that I was right about it from the beginning.

jokes.text: Remember that time that you told me you'd be there for me if I ever needed you? / I don't think that was me.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn appeals to a promise Alexis supposedly made about being there for her.
jokes.punchline: Alexis flatly denies ever making the promise, which is both plausible and a perfect encapsulation of her self-centered character.
jokes.explanation: Works because of Alexis's specific brand of oblivious self-interest. The denial is perfectly calibrated — not 'I never said that' but 'I don't think that was me,' leaving just enough ambiguity that it's even funnier. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You promised me you'd always be there for me.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I never said that.

jokes.text: Seriously. A nighttime couples massage, what is this, 1985?!
jokes.setup: Johnny points out Roland and Jocelyn asked everyone at the table for babysitting except them.
jokes.punchline: Alexis completely misses the actual slight (being excluded from babysitting duty) and instead mocks the couples massage as dated.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's total misdirection of her offense is a nice character beat. The 1985 reference is a little thin, but her priorities being completely wrong is the real joke and that lands.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We need a babysitter tonight because Jocelyn and I have our monthly couples massage.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And the masseuse insists on working on us simultaneously, completely naked, in the same room.

jokes.text: No! They asked everyone at the table except us, we're sitting right here!
jokes.setup: Alexis is offended by the wrong thing (the massage comment). Johnny is actually offended by the right thing but reveals it sounds absurd — they're hurt about NOT being asked to babysit.
jokes.punchline: Johnny is indignant about being passed over for babysitting duty, which inverts all normal parental feelings and reveals his wounded pride.
jokes.explanation: The inversion works — being insulted about not being asked to babysit is inherently funny. Sets up the B-plot engine well. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're not inviting us?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, this is so last minute!

jokes.text: They're not going to ask someone of your age, John.
jokes.setup: Johnny is indignant about being excluded from babysitting consideration.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response is framed as sympathy but is actually a casual dig at Johnny's age, delivered with complete Moira nonchalance.
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — she thinks she's being comforting while landing an insult. The delivery and character-specificity elevate what would otherwise be a simple age joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying they didn't like my outfit?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, they didn't invite us to the party.

jokes.text: 'Raised' might be a stretch. Our nursery was in a different wing of the house. / It was a design flaw. / I'm a light sleeper.
jokes.setup: Johnny defensively says 'We raised two kids' to prove their parenting credentials.
jokes.punchline: Moira undermines the claim, then they both escalate into increasingly absurd rationalizations — design flaw, light sleeping — that reveal their terrible parenting while each trying to justify it.
jokes.explanation: This exchange is excellent Schitt's Creek writing. The escalation from 'raised might be a stretch' to the nursery design flaw to Johnny's light sleeping is a perfectly structured three-beat joke where each justification is funnier and more damning than the last. Both characters are deeply integrated — Moira's grandiose self-pity and Johnny's desperate rationalization are completely authentic. The 'I'm a light sleeper' button especially lands.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're actually very good parents.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We really are not.

jokes.text: John, you're not still working on that cumbersome laundry hamper? / I told you, it's a playpen, Moira. / I was hoping you were joking.
jokes.setup: Moira sees Johnny assembling something and assumes it's a laundry hamper.
jokes.punchline: It's a playpen — which Moira had hoped was a joke — implying she's horrified by the domestic task and/or the object's quality.
jokes.explanation: The confusion between a playpen and a laundry hamper implies both the object's sad quality and Moira's complete domestic incompetence. 'I was hoping you were joking' is a very Moira line. Solid character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We believed in letting the children find their own way.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We were very busy with our careers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was dealing with my skin issues.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We had a very active social calendar.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David had already basically raised himself by age seven.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis was very independent from the womb.

jokes.text: That's an argument I'm willing to lose.
jokes.setup: Johnny explains he volunteered to babysit to prove Roland and Jocelyn wrong about their parenting capability.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately concedes she'd be fine losing the argument that they can't parent — because she'd rather not babysit.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best lines in the episode. Moira's willingness to concede she's a bad parent just to avoid responsibility is delivered with complete sincerity and it's devastating and funny. Exceptional economy — six words capturing her entire character. Could only come from Moira. This is the show at its best.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm assembling this crib for David and Patrick's baby.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How delightful. I do love a newborn. The smell, the sounds, the... constant need for attention.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're going to help babysit, right?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Babysit? Darling, I thought we were just admiring from a distance. A very great distance. Preferably with a closed door between us.

jokes.text: The Lifetime Network is re-airing the movie I did with Joyce DeWitt. 'Not Without My Cousin.'
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she can't help with the baby — she has plans at Ronnie's.
jokes.punchline: Her plans are to watch her own Lifetime movie — titled 'Not Without My Cousin,' a parody of 'Not Without My Daughter' — co-starring the very specific Joyce DeWitt from Three's Company.
jokes.explanation: The specificity is doing all the work here. 'Not Without My Cousin' as a title is funny on its own — a diminished stakes parody of a serious film. Adding Joyce DeWitt (a very specific B-list celebrity choice) and the Lebanon detail paints a perfect absurdist picture of Moira's career. The joke earns rewatch because you catch the Lebanon reference later.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to prove ourselves to this town. Show them we're not just a bunch of wealthy people who fell on hard times.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm tired.

jokes.text: Yeah, we were there most weeknights. For at least a couple of hours. Unless we were out for the evening. Yeah, or jetting around. Or if it was a really long day.
jokes.setup: Johnny insists 'I'm not the least bit concerned' about their parenting ability and he and Moira begin to reassure each other.
jokes.punchline: Their reassurances immediately devolve into a list of all the reasons they actually weren't present parents, each caveat undercutting the last with cheerful casualness.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed escalation where each new excuse is funnier than the last and they're ping-ponging between each other in cheerful agreement, completely oblivious to how damning their list is. 'Or if it was a really long day' as the final button is especially good — the mundane reason landing after the more exotic 'jetting around.' This is the show's warmth-wrapped-in-darkness at its finest.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's called 'The Crows Have Eyes III: The Crowening.' And I may not have mentioned it.

jokes.text: Besides, if Roland can look after a baby, anyone can.
jokes.setup: Johnny is reassuring himself he can handle babysitting.
jokes.punchline: His logic for why he'll be fine is that Roland — implied to be barely competent — does it, so the bar must be low enough for anyone.
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient Roland burn that gets the laugh without overstaying. Not particularly original — 'if X can do it, anyone can' is a familiar structure — but executed cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're excellent parents. We've always been there for David and Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, except for that boarding school incident.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That was character building.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And the time we forgot to pick them up from camp.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We were in Monaco.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: For a week.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A very important week.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And then there was the nanny situation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Which nanny? There were several.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: it just would've never occurred to us to ask you.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn thanks Johnny profusely for agreeing to babysit.
jokes.punchline: She casually admits that asking the Roses literally never crossed their minds — an enormous indirect insult delivered with complete warmth and sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The total sincerity of Jocelyn's insult makes it land. She's not being mean — she genuinely would never have thought of them, which is somehow worse. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How hard can it be? Roland's been doing it for years.

jokes.text: and if he does find himself completely lost, and believe me, he will...
jokes.setup: Roland is ostensibly reassuring Jocelyn that Johnny will be fine.
jokes.punchline: He immediately undermines his own reassurance with 'and believe me, he will,' expressing zero confidence in Johnny while pretending to be supportive.
jokes.explanation: The parenthetical aside from Roland is a reliable structural move — the fake reassurance that's actually a burn. Works for Roland's character and gets a solid laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Don't you worry, Johnny. You're gonna be a natural at this parenting thing. I can tell.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Just remember, it's not rocket science. You just follow the baby manual, and you'll be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Although, knowing you, you'll probably need to read it a few times. Maybe more than a few times.

jokes.text: Wow, Moira. Looking so elegant for a night of babysitting. Just dressed to the nines in choking hazards.
jokes.setup: Moira appears in full elaborate costume/jewelry as she's heading to Ronnie's for a screening.
jokes.punchline: Roland points out her elaborate jewelry in the context of a babysitting environment is a collection of choking hazards — framing high fashion as a safety violation.
jokes.explanation: 'Dressed to the nines in choking hazards' is a genuinely great line — the reframe of Moira's glamour through baby-safety optics is unexpected and specific. The contrast between Moira's oblivious elegance and the domestic setting is a recurring visual joke of the show, and this is a particularly sharp articulation of it. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I don't trust him, you know. Never have.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know you are.

jokes.text: Sadly, Jocelyn, I won't be here this evening to intercede.
jokes.setup: Roland has just made the choking hazard comment, implying concern about the baby's safety with the Roses.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response acknowledges the implicit danger and expresses 'sadness' about not being there to intervene — while showing zero actual concern and clearly prioritizing her TV plans.
jokes.explanation: The faux-tragic tone of 'Sadly' combined with complete indifference to actual baby safety is very Moira. The word 'intercede' — such an elevated, formal word for a babysitting situation — adds to the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's a lot of jewelry. If you leaned over a baby, you could put its eye out.

jokes.text: Oh wow, so it's just gonna be Johnny, alone with our precious miracle baby?
jokes.setup: Moira casually mentions she won't be there — Jocelyn only now processes that Johnny will be alone.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's delayed panic registers audibly — 'our precious miracle baby' as a descriptor heightens the stakes comedically, and her realization lands as a comic beat.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — the dawning horror paired with the overly precious phrase 'miracle baby' works. Not exceptional but serves the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Wait, Moira's not going to be there? Oh no. Oh no no no. Who's going to keep Johnny occupied? Who's going to... I mean, he's very charming, but he needs... he needs guidance. Structure. Someone to tell him when he's being ridiculous, which is constantly.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I can't be responsible for that. I have enough on my plate. I'm very busy. I have a lot of things happening in my life right now.

jokes.text: I'll be thinking about all of you during my massage.
jokes.setup: The baby situation is clearly chaotic and Jocelyn is starting to panic.
jokes.punchline: Roland offers the completely useless 'thought' of thinking about them — during his massage — as though this constitutes meaningful support.
jokes.explanation: Roland's cheerful uselessness is a character staple. The specificity of the massage context makes the hollow offer funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Johnny, life's gonna throw curveballs at you. Sometimes you gotta just swing and miss, and sometimes you gotta take one for the team.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I have no idea what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: No, no need for that, Roland.
jokes.setup: Roland offers to think of them during his massage.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat dismissal — 'no need for that' — as if Roland's stray thoughts could somehow cause harm is a small but perfect deadpan button.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's measured exasperation is very Eugene Levy. The implication that Roland's thoughts during a massage are an unwanted intrusion lands quietly but effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I didn't have any shots.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, maybe one.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay, fine, I had a whole tray.

jokes.text: Okay Johnny, just don't do anything I wouldn't do. / Boop boop be boop!
jokes.setup: Roland is leaving Johnny in charge and offers parting advice.
jokes.punchline: 'Don't do anything I wouldn't do' from Roland is almost no restriction at all given his character. The capper — 'Boop boop be boop!' — as a parting scat-style farewell is a perfectly absurdist Roland moment.
jokes.explanation: Roland's advice being functionally meaningless given his own lack of standards is a good implicit joke. The 'boop boop be boop' as an exit is pure Roland absurdism — his tendency toward performative goofiness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where exactly in Schitt's Creek am I supposed to find luxury items?

jokes.text: Fine, once. / Twice. / Fine, I had a tray in Cancun.
jokes.setup: Patrick offers David party shots and asks if he's ever had one. David initially denies it.
jokes.punchline: David's rapid capitulation from 'No' to 'once' to 'twice' to 'a tray in Cancun' is a perfectly structured self-incriminating escalation.
jokes.explanation: Textbook escalation construction. The three-step structure (no → once → twice → Cancun tray) is perfectly paced and the specificity of 'a tray in Cancun' is the exact right image to cap it. Completely David — his performative dignity collapsing beat by beat. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, what high school did you go to?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Because based on the things you've said, it sounds like you went to some sort of sex academy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I went to a normal high school.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, apparently at your normal high school, everyone was just constantly making out in the hallways and the cafeteria was basically a strip club.

jokes.text: Models and teen actors are just gonna lounge around in their bras and thongs, and take Polaroids of themselves all night? / What was your high school experience like?
jokes.setup: David dismisses Patrick's high school slumber party theme and sarcastically describes what it would involve.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's genuine, curious 'What was your high school experience like?' reframes David's sarcastic hypothetical as apparently autobiographical.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent setup/punchline construction. David's description sounds absurd as satire but Patrick's response implies it might be David's actual past, which is both a great character reveal and very funny. The pivot to 'What was YOUR high school like?' as a mirror question adds another layer. One of the sharper exchanges in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need a salad bowl of E.

jokes.text: Besides, where are we going to find a salad bowl of E this last minute?
jokes.setup: David is dismissing the logistics of his own alternate theme ideas.
jokes.punchline: The casual, practical framing of sourcing a 'salad bowl of E' (MDMA) as a logistical party-planning challenge — not a moral one — reveals David's entire background.
jokes.explanation: This is a standout line. The unit of measurement — 'salad bowl' — is perfectly chosen (domestic object repurposed for something completely depraved) and the framing as a logistical rather than ethical concern is precisely right for David. High originality, exceptional economy, completely character-specific. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've been working through some things in therapy, and my therapist suggested I confront my past. So I'm going to that high school themed party.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's like walking back into the lion's den, except the lion is wearing a letterman jacket and has a regrettable amount of body glitter.

jokes.text: It's just that my parents have spent like, an astounding amount of money on therapy to try to do that already. So forgive me for not wanting to run back into the fire.
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests the party is a fun opportunity for David to 'rewrite his past a little bit.'
jokes.punchline: David reveals his parents have spent heavily on therapy to help him do exactly that — and the therapy hasn't worked — making his reluctance darkly funny and specific.
jokes.explanation: The revelation that extensive expensive therapy has failed to resolve David's high school trauma is funny and sad in equal measure. 'Running back into the fire' is a good image. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going as a Playboy Bunny. It's iconic, it's fun, it's sexy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's also a costume that screams 'I have no thoughts.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, Hugh Hefner had a very successful life.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, by dating women who were half his age, which is somehow half his age.

jokes.text: Honey, I'm home. / Oh, shouldn't I be the one saying that?
jokes.setup: Ted arrives home cheerfully saying 'honey, I'm home.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects him — she should be the one saying it since it's her apartment — which gently establishes the domestic dynamic and gives Alexis a small moment of territorial pride.
jokes.explanation: Light, pleasant joke. The reversal is predictable but the character beat is charming. Functional opener for the Ted/Alexis scene.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to make a gesture, and it's going to be very clear what I mean.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: This is what I think about you... *makes aggressive cutting gesture* ...neutering.

jokes.text: Except it's Neuterpalooza week at work, and I still have two days of spaying and neutering ahead of me. / Let's stop saying 'neutering', 'cause it's killing my buzz!
jokes.setup: Ted is celebrating what sounds like the end of the week.
jokes.punchline: It's 'Neuterpalooza week' — a comedically branded event at the vet clinic — and Alexis's reaction to the word 'neutering' killing her romantic buzz is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: 'Neuterpalooza' is an inspired bit of naming — the Lollapalooza suffix applied to a week of animal castration is genuinely funny. Alexis's objection to the word is both character-true and timing-driven. Good pairing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A baby is crying? But we have reservations!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, babies cry. That's what they do.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, not on my schedule. I assumed they came with some sort of manual. Like a dishwasher.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's my dad's housecoat from storage. I was thinking we could go as Hef, R.I.P., and a Playboy Bunny.
jokes.setup: Alexis admires Ted's silky robe and asks about it.
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals it's Johnny's housecoat and proposes going as Hugh Hefner and a Playboy Bunny to a high school slumber party, with 'R.I.P.' appended naturally mid-sentence.
jokes.explanation: The matter-of-fact 'R.I.P.' mid-pitch is the best part — Ted's earnest reverence for Hefner while suggesting they dress as him is sweet and slightly absurd. The costume concept for a slumber party is also inherently wrong in a funny way.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God. For a moment, I thought it was you.

jokes.text: My best friend, Summer, used to date Hef in high school, which isn't as weird as it sounds, 'cause he was like, a very young 77.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions the Hef costume and Alexis connects it to her past.
jokes.punchline: 'Which isn't as weird as it sounds, 'cause he was like, a very young 77' — the defense of a 77-year-old dating a high schooler as 'not that weird' because he was 'young for his age' is gloriously wrongheaded Alexis logic.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. 'A very young 77' is an exceptional construction — it's a contradiction that only makes sense in Alexis's world, where the logic is internally consistent even while being completely insane. The specificity of 77 (not 'old' or '80') makes it funnier. Deeply character-specific — only Alexis Rose could say this with a straight face and mean it as a defense. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you have any rubber gloves?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: These are Hermès, David.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just need something to protect my hands.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they wouldn't fit you anyway.

jokes.text: Snip, snip, Mr. Mullens!
jokes.setup: Ted has just agreed to push his morning appointments, meaning he'll be doing neutering surgery with a shaky hungover hand.
jokes.punchline: Cut to Ted on the phone — apparently scheduling/confirming a neutering appointment — delivering 'Snip, snip, Mr. Mullens!' with chipper veterinary professionalism, immediately undercutting his romantic sacrifice.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition cut — from a tender romantic moment to 'Snip, snip, Mr. Mullens!' — is a perfectly timed visual/structural joke. The cheerful professionalism of 'snip, snip' alongside the appointment-holder's name makes this specific and absurd in the best way. High quotability. The scene cut IS the joke, which is good visual comedy craft.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It says here to flip the baby every two hours.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You have to flip the baby?

jokes.text: You realize the bebe is crying. / I do, yes. / Isn't it scheduled to be dormant by now?
jokes.setup: Moira is about to leave. The baby is crying.
jokes.punchline: Moira asks if the baby is 'scheduled to be dormant' — treating the infant like a household appliance on a timer, with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: 'Scheduled to be dormant' is an outstanding line. The word 'dormant' applied to a baby is unexpected, clinical, and reveals Moira's profound disconnect from childcare reality. This is quintessential Moira Rose vocabulary applied to a situation it has no business being applied to. One of the episode's strongest lines in terms of originality and character integration. Very high quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't know if Johnny's ever been alone with the baby before. What if he forgets to feed her? What if he forgets she exists?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, I think I can manage for a few hours.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, when our nanny had to leave her daughter's wedding early last year, the baby didn't eat for two days.

jokes.text: Oh, it was the bebe. I'm so relieved!
jokes.setup: Johnny realizes the issue might be in the diaper. There's an implied smell.
jokes.punchline: Moira expresses relief that it was the baby causing the smell — implying she genuinely wasn't sure if the smell was the baby or something else — which is both absurd and revealing of her disconnect.
jokes.explanation: Moira's relief at confirming the smell source is funny for what it implies about her expectations. The 'bebe' pronunciation adds to the Moira brand.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The ridiculous premise of cousins backpacking through Lebanon

jokes.text: They're Tom Ford, John, no! / And they wouldn't fit you anyway.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Moira to grab the rubber gloves to handle the diaper situation.
jokes.punchline: Moira refuses because her gloves are Tom Ford — a luxury brand — and then adds the unnecessary insult that they wouldn't fit Johnny anyway.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira. The idea of Tom Ford cleaning gloves existing and being protected is absurd luxury. The additional gratuitous insult that they 'wouldn't fit you anyway' — not even necessary but delivered as if it's also relevant — is a great escalation button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Never have I ever... pretended to understand what someone was talking about just to seem smart.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David! Really? You're really going to do this right now?

jokes.text: it's saying here that you should... wipe counter-clockwise direction... flip it, if you can flip it. / The baby? / It doesn't say.
jokes.setup: Moira is reading from the baby binder to help Johnny with the diaper change.
jokes.punchline: The instruction to 'flip it' is ambiguous enough that Johnny asks 'the baby?' and Moira can only reply 'it doesn't say' — the binder's silence on this crucial point is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the institutional helplessness — the baby binder, supposed to be a comprehensive guide, leaves them genuinely uncertain whether they should flip the infant. 'It doesn't say' as a sincere answer is a great deadpan button. The Roses' competence gap expressed through bureaucratic dependence is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: My mom used to play this game. She'd drink whatever was in front of her.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So I'm basically trying to honor her memory and also get drunk as fast as possible.

jokes.text: It's not like the old days when we could just call Adelina, and have her rush home from her daughter's wedding.
jokes.setup: Moira worries Johnny won't cope and references how they used to handle parenting emergencies.
jokes.punchline: Their coping mechanism was calling the nanny and pulling her out of her own daughter's wedding — presented as a totally reasonable thing to have done.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'her daughter's wedding' is what makes this. It's not just 'we called the nanny' — the nanny was at a significant life event and they pulled her out. The casual way Moira frames this as the obvious solution is brilliantly revealing of the old Rose household's priorities. Strong dark subversive beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Never have I ever... used a bidet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not going to work because I've done almost everything.

jokes.text: That's when the action happens anyway, when my cousin and I begin our backpack through Lebanon.
jokes.setup: Moira decides to stay until the first commercial break before going to Ronnie's.
jokes.punchline: She describes her own movie's plot — backpacking through Lebanon with her cousin — as though it's an objectively exciting piece of cinema, delivered with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the movie setup. The backpacking-through-Lebanon detail adds absurdist texture to what is clearly a C-grade Lifetime film. Moira's pride in her work is consistent and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How long is this game going to go on?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, but I'm guessing until everyone's tired of it. Which, based on what I'm watching, should be right about now.

jokes.text: Hmm, never have I ever pretended to be a pizza delivery person so I could get into Jared Leto's Halloween party.
jokes.setup: The party has started playing 'Never Have I Ever.' David takes his turn.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of the method (pizza delivery disguise), the target (Jared Leto's Halloween party), and David's casual ownership of this action reveals his entire former life in one sentence.
jokes.explanation: The specificity is extraordinary — not just a celebrity party, but Jared Leto's, not just getting in, but via pizza delivery disguise. David delivering this as his 'Never Have I Ever' contribution tells you everything about his former life. Strong originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Never have I ever... faked an orgasm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm out. Last Tuesday.

jokes.text: You're a bitch.
jokes.setup: David's 'Never Have I Ever' entry (Jared Leto's party via pizza delivery) clearly applies to Stevie, who must drink.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, affectionate 'You're a bitch' while presumably drinking is both an insult and a term of endearment, and the bluntness is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — two words that carry enormous character weight. Stevie and David's friendship is completely captured in this exchange. The delivery would be everything in performance but the line itself is efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I drank your coolers. I'm sorry, I just... I needed to take the edge off.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh God, that was a mistake. That was a huge mistake. It tastes like a broken dream mixed with regret and... is that coconut?

jokes.text: My mom was engaged to two pizza delivery guys. Can that count? 'Cause I am really thirsty.
jokes.setup: The 'Never Have I Ever' about pizza delivery person infiltration puts Twyla in an awkward position.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's mom having been engaged to TWO pizza delivery guys (separately) — and Twyla's desperate hope that this adjacent experience allows her to drink — is a perfect Twyla moment.
jokes.explanation: Classic Twyla — another dark, casually delivered family revelation about her chaotic home life, this time her mother's serial engagements to pizza delivery men. The 'can that count' plea is both funny and slightly sad, which is exactly Twyla's register. 'I am really thirsty' as the actual motivation is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Never have I ever... [long pause] Sorry, I'm just trying to think of something I haven't done.
jokes.setup: It's David's turn again in Never Have I Ever.
jokes.punchline: David's extended pause reveals that he genuinely cannot think of something he hasn't done — making the game's premise collapse under the weight of his past.
jokes.explanation: The pause being the joke is well-executed. David having apparently done everything is both funny and consistent with his backstory. 'I'm just trying to think of something I haven't done' lands as a great character-comedy line.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I do want to continue the bottle game. I'm just being honest that I also want this party to end.

jokes.text: Because never have I ever been so bored.
jokes.setup: The game is stalling because David can't think of something he hasn't done.
jokes.punchline: Patrick hijacks the format to express his boredom with the game itself, using the game's structure as a delivery mechanism for a meta-complaint.
jokes.explanation: Using the game's 'never have I ever' structure to meta-comment on the game is a clean, efficient joke. Patrick's dry wit cuts through well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not putting my phone away. What if Alexis needs to reach me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I'm literally right here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, you could leave. And then you'd need to reach me.

jokes.text: I didn't expect that you'd be drinking quite so much.
jokes.setup: Patrick acknowledges he suggested Never Have I Ever but David is getting very drunk.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's innocent disclaimer that he 'didn't expect you'd be drinking quite so much' is a polite way of saying David has apparently done everything the game can propose.
jokes.explanation: Solid callback. The implication — that David has apparently done everything — is funnier for being understated and delivered politely.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm great! I'm fantastic! Best night of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Am I? ...Maybe. Possibly. But that doesn't make it untrue!

jokes.text: Eating out of the garbage? / Okay, I did that in front of you last night.
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests something as a 'Never Have I Ever' prompt.
jokes.punchline: David confirms he did that — in front of Patrick — just last night. The casualness of 'I did that in front of you last night' implies Patrick witnessed it without comment until now.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'in front of you last night' is what makes this — implying Patrick watched David eat from the garbage and they just... moved on. A great snapshot of their relationship dynamic. David's casualness about the revelation is perfectly deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David! Yes! Yes!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Come here, you!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa, Ted—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no, no, it's fine. The bottle said so.

jokes.text: After you used all of my booze for your punch, the only alcohol left in my fridge was a few of your rum, raspberry, coconut coolers. So I polished them off, and... it tasted like burnt plastic, and I regretted it instantly.
jokes.setup: Ted arrives at the party and explains his pre-party drinking situation.
jokes.punchline: Ted drank Alexis's pink coolers out of desperation, and his honest assessment — 'tasted like burnt plastic, and I regretted it instantly' — is delivered with the same sincerity he'd give a medical opinion.
jokes.explanation: The progression from using Alexis's booze for punch → being left with pink coolers → honest review of the coolers is well-structured. 'Burnt plastic' as a flavor descriptor is funny and specific. Ted's earnest honesty makes the insult land softly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need to file a formal complaint. Ted has been calling me 'big guy' repeatedly, and I find it deeply inappropriate and unprofessional.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: David, it's just a friendly nickname, big guy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That is exactly what I'm talking about. And I am not your 'big guy.' I am David Rose. I have a last name. I have a business. I have standards.

jokes.text: I'm not sure if it's just the punch, but... he could get it!
jokes.setup: Ted arrives at the party enthusiastically, wearing his housecoat and slippers, and is immediately charming and energetic.
jokes.punchline: Stevie, watching Ted's entrance, delivers a flat assessment of his attractiveness with a caveat about whether it's real or punch-induced.
jokes.explanation: 'He could get it' from deadpan Stevie is a great comedic pivot. The disclaimer ('I'm not sure if it's just the punch') is an honest self-check that makes it funnier. Very Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The pigtails were a misstep. I mean, we're talking about astrophysicists here, not a 1970s game show hostess.

jokes.text: Stevie, that's disgusting.
jokes.setup: Stevie says Ted 'could get it' — Ted being Alexis's boyfriend and David's quasi-brother-in-law.
jokes.punchline: David's disgusted response plays as protective of both Alexis and his own sense of family propriety, from a man who ate from garbage last night.
jokes.explanation: David's protective disgust is funny given his own moral flexibility. Short, punchy, functional. Not exceptional but lands in context.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You may want to change that. You look like you've been attacked by a very small, very weak predator.

jokes.text: Hey! Do you wanna put Emir away for a hot sec, so this game can keep going, and the party can end soon?
jokes.setup: Someone at the party is on their phone with someone named Emir.
jokes.punchline: David's request frames the party ending 'soon' as the goal — the host's boyfriend wanting the party to be over is a funny inversion of party hosting energy.
jokes.explanation: 'So the party can end soon' is the funniest part — David's barely concealed contempt for the party he supposedly supports. Consistent characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is clearly a manufacturing defect. The warranty on this model must have expired.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, I'm going to need you to contact the supplier immediately. This unit is completely non-functional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, it's a baby. Babies spit up.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, your baby is malfunctioning, and frankly, I'm not surprised given the quality of the raw materials you provided.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm good. I don't wanna kiss anyone here. My sister's sitting in the circle.
jokes.setup: Someone asks David to join the spin-the-bottle game.
jokes.punchline: David lists his reasons for not joining, and 'my sister's in the circle' is presented as equally valid a reason as not wanting to kiss anyone — which inadvertently highlights the whole incest-avoidance concern as a real consideration.
jokes.explanation: The casual listing of 'my sister's in the circle' alongside 'I don't want to kiss anyone' is a funny structural beat — the gravity of the sibling concern being equal to general disinterest.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried feeding the baby shellfish? I read somewhere that it builds character. Or was it builds cholesterol? Either way, the baby needs to develop a more refined palate.

jokes.text: Babe, did you rig this? / Maybe I did, and maybe I did!
jokes.setup: The bottle in spin-the-bottle appears to have landed on Alexis for Ted.
jokes.punchline: Ted's delighted double confirmation — 'maybe I did, and maybe I did!' — is a joyful confession that's funny for its exuberance and the fact that he rigged a party game.
jokes.explanation: Ted's enthusiasm for rigging a party game to kiss his girlfriend is charming and sweet. The repeated 'maybe I did' lands on his character's energy well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the baby has crawling pneumonia!

jokes.text: All right, who's the lucky lady?
jokes.setup: Ted's spin has landed — but it's landed on a man (Patrick), not Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Ted's oblivious 'Who's the lucky lady?' while the bottle is pointing at Patrick sets up the kiss that follows — his assumption that it landed on a woman is the comedic misdirect.
jokes.explanation: The misdirect works because it's innocent rather than malicious — Ted's genuine assumption before the reveal. Sets up the Patrick kiss perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't mean to say 'I told you so,' but I did tell you so. And I'm not one to gloat, but if I were, this would be a very good time for it.

jokes.text: That's offensive. / Okay, anyway, re-spin! / Yeah, it actually looks like it sorta landed between two people. / No, no, no, no! I know exactly who it landed on.
jokes.setup: After Ted's 'lucky lady' comment, Patrick objects. Alexis tries to call a re-spin. Ted insists he knows who it landed on.
jokes.punchline: The rapid layering of Patrick's offense, Alexis's escape hatch, and Ted's certainty creates a perfect comedic escalation before the inevitable Patrick-Ted kiss.
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire back-and-forth is well-constructed — three characters with three different objectives colliding efficiently. The structure anticipates the kiss perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that's a good one, Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, for you.

jokes.text: Ted kisses Patrick enthusiastically at the spin-the-bottle game.
jokes.setup: The bottle has landed on Patrick; everyone expects awkwardness.
jokes.punchline: Ted goes in enthusiastically and fully — 'Man, come here, big guy!' — turning what could be an awkward moment into an over-committed comedic spectacle.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Ted's total commitment — he calls Patrick 'big guy' and goes in fully. The audience reaction ('Oh my God') and the prolonged nature of the kiss are the escalation. Warm, inclusive comedy that the show handles beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's so funny how David and Stevie are like, so above everyone else, you know? Like, they're just too cool for normal social interaction. It's very annoying.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not being judgmental. I'm just observing that they're being judgmental. Which is different.

jokes.text: It looks like the punch needs a refill. / Oh hey, I could use a refill too, big guy. / Okay, I don't know where 'big guy' is coming from, but it needs to stop.
jokes.setup: After the kiss, David is processing by making punch-related excuses to leave.
jokes.punchline: Ted calls David 'big guy' (a nickname presumably transferred from the Patrick kiss) and David immediately shuts down the nickname with flat irritation.
jokes.explanation: The 'big guy' running gag starting here works because it's transferred from the Patrick kiss context. David's flat 'I don't know where big guy is coming from, but it needs to stop' is excellent character work — his controlled irritation vs Ted's breezy obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're like a disease-carrying fly that somehow got into a Hermès store and thought it belonged there.

jokes.text: Oh, if it weren't for Joyce DeWitt insisting on wearing those pigtails, this movie could've been a hit! We're supposed to be astrophysicists, it just takes you out of it.
jokes.setup: Moira is at Ronnie's watching 'Not Without My Cousin' and offering commentary.
jokes.punchline: Moira's film criticism focuses on Joyce DeWitt's pigtails as the obstacle to greatness — not the Lebanon-cousin premise — and the detail that they're playing astrophysicists makes the pigtail objection both funnier and more absurd.
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent payoff to the earlier movie setup. The layers work perfectly: the movie is clearly terrible, they're playing astrophysicists (insane for a Lifetime travel film), Joyce DeWitt's pigtails are apparently the main critical issue, and Moira's self-serving review places all blame on DeWitt. 'It just takes you out of it' as a film criticism cliché applied to this context is great. One of the best Moira moments in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, we need to talk about the kissing incident.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, it was just one time—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: One time. One. Single. Time. David, one time is all it takes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, but—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: One time, David. One time!

jokes.text: To think this little thing could... create so much destruction.
jokes.setup: The baby has just had its diaper changed. Johnny marvels at the situation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's philosophical reflection on the baby's 'destruction' — really just a diaper situation — frames a mundane parenting moment in dramatic terms.
jokes.explanation: Decent beat. The overblown language for a diaper change is standard but effective. Eugene Levy's delivery would elevate this significantly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What if I told you that you had to choose between me and David?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm right here if you want a third option.

jokes.text: Aren't you the Sephardic Mr. Clean!
jokes.setup: Johnny has successfully changed the diaper and cleaned up.
jokes.punchline: Moira's compliment is 'the Sephardic Mr. Clean' — an unexpected, hyper-specific pop culture reference that says more about Moira's associative mind than anything else.
jokes.explanation: 'Sephardic Mr. Clean' is a very Moira construction — culturally specific, slightly off, delivered as a genuine compliment. The reference makes no real sense as a compliment but that's exactly why it works. Highly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, so now I'm jealous? I'm not jealous. Why would I be jealous? If you want to kiss him, just kiss him. Go ahead. Kiss him right now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Prove it to me. Kiss him right now and I'll believe that nothing is going on.

jokes.text: Well, I did the kid, but I'm not doing the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Moira praises Johnny for the diaper. There's implied mess beyond just the diaper situation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny draws a firm line — he'll handle the baby but not the secondary cleanup — which is both reasonable and funny as a new parenting boundary.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's boundary-setting is a solid button. The simplicity and economy of the line works well.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, that was... nice. You're a good kisser.

jokes.text: It appears you may need a change yourself, Mr. Rose. What is that on your shoulder?
jokes.setup: Johnny has just finished dealing with the baby's mess.
jokes.punchline: Moira notices baby spit-up on Johnny's shoulder — he's been marked by the baby too — which is both a comeuppance and a visual gag.
jokes.explanation: 'Need a change yourself' is a classic spit-up reveal structure. Moira's formal 'Mr. Rose' elevates a standard moment. Works fine.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That was the most sexually charged moment of my entire life. I'm not even exaggerating. I think I saw colors I've never seen before. I'm pretty sure I left my body for a second there.

jokes.text: And he's spit up in the playpen! Ew! That cannot be good, John! Its body is ejecting things, what did you do to it?!
jokes.setup: The baby has spit up — first on Johnny, then also in the playpen.
jokes.punchline: Moira's escalating horror culminates in 'its body is ejecting things, what did you do to it?!' — treating normal infant spit-up as evidence of abuse or injury.
jokes.explanation: 'Its body is ejecting things' is a great Moira construction — clinical language ('ejecting') for a biological process she finds horrifying. Her immediate accusation of Johnny is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, at least the baby's finally quiet.

jokes.text: Well, perhaps you've swaddled its abdomen too tightly. Or maybe it's allergic to something. John, did you feed it shellfish?
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to diagnose why the baby spit up.
jokes.punchline: After some reasonable guesses (swaddling, allergies), she lands on 'did you feed it shellfish?' — an absurd escalation given Johnny has been in charge for roughly 20 minutes.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from reasonable to absurd works perfectly. 'Did you feed it shellfish?' is a great button — the implication that Johnny might have sourced seafood for an infant in the twenty minutes since they left is wonderfully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think what we've learned is that our nanny was severely underpaid.

jokes.text: I don't want to say I told you so, but this is an infelicitous burden.
jokes.setup: The baby situation is spiraling and Moira is now stuck helping instead of going to Ronnie's.
jokes.punchline: 'Infelicitous burden' as a description for babysitting a temporarily gassy baby is a Moira-vocabulary escalation. 'I don't want to say I told you so, but' — and then she does.
jokes.explanation: 'Infelicitous burden' — the word choice is doing the heavy lifting. Moira's vocabulary in mundane situations is reliably one of the show's funniest devices.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think we did a marvelous job. The children are still alive, aren't they?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Barely. They were covered in marker.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Patina, darling. It gives them character. My parents raised me the same way, and look how I turned out.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's exactly what I'm worried about.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think benign neglect builds resilience. It's a parenting philosophy.

jokes.text: 'Spit happens.' / Very clever. / For Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Moira finds a note in the baby binder from Jocelyn that reads 'Spit happens.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Very clever. For Jocelyn.' is a perfect double-edged compliment — acknowledging the joke's cleverness while simultaneously placing a very low ceiling on that cleverness.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best lines in the episode. 'Very clever. For Jocelyn.' is a perfectly structured three-word burn — the comma and 'for Jocelyn' qualifier do everything. It acknowledges wit while immediately damning it with faint praise in the most specific possible way. Could only be Moira. Exceptional economy, extremely quotable, and the structure (compliment → devastating qualifier) is flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Her best acting was believing we were really cousins.

jokes.text: You remind me of me at a high school house party judging all the attractive and popular kids for being attractive and popular.
jokes.setup: Stevie finds David and Patrick off to the side, clearly not participating in the party.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's observation positions them as the exact type of party wallflowers they used to be — and positions the current party guests as the 'attractive and popular kids' they resented.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's self-aware observation is sharp and well-delivered. It also gently mocks all three of them simultaneously, which is efficient. The specificity of 'for being attractive and popular' as the stated reason for judging is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: How did you get the baby to sleep?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was talking about Johnny.

jokes.text: Now, as an attractive and popular kid at a house party, I can confidently say, it's very fun.
jokes.setup: Stevie compares them to wallflowers judging attractive popular kids.
jokes.punchline: David immediately claims the 'attractive and popular' mantle and offers a satirically hollow endorsement of fun — while standing apart from the party and not participating.
jokes.explanation: David's immediate self-positioning as attractive and popular — while obviously behaving like anything but — is good character comedy. The deadpan 'it's very fun' while clearly having no fun is precise.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So, did everything go okay?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it was absolutely delightful. The baby came out like a little soufflé that had been slightly overworked.

jokes.text: Mm, things got effed, and we're not having fun anymore.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks what's going on with the group on the side.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's blunt, expletive-adjacent summary of the situation — 'things got effed' — is efficient and characteristically Stevie.
jokes.explanation: Short, functional. Stevie's flatness is the joke. Works but doesn't distinguish itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: We should do this more often.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Let's have that idea massaged away.

jokes.text: You're like a tsetse fly.
jokes.setup: Ted has just cheerfully announced he's having a great time.
jokes.punchline: David's response — 'you're like a tsetse fly' — is an oddly specific, left-field insult that implies Ted is an agent of discomfort/disease without being a standard insult.
jokes.explanation: 'Tsetse fly' is an inspired and unexpected insult choice — exotic, specific, carries connotations of being an unwelcome presence and carrier of disruption. The specificity and unexpectedness are what make it work. Very David.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I gotta say, he's a lot easier to be around when he's sleeping.

jokes.text: Mmm, was it the time you kissed my boyfriend? Or... was it the time you kissed my boyfriend? / I think it was the time you kissed her boyfriend.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks what he did wrong at the party. The group processes the spin-the-bottle Ted-Patrick kiss.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's repeated 'was it the time you kissed my boyfriend?' and Patrick's interjecting 'I think it was the time you kissed her boyfriend' — providing the same fact from a different angle — creates a funny double-assessment of a single incident.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'the time you kissed my boyfriend' (as if it's a recurring historical event rather than something that just happened) combined with Patrick's helpfully redundant clarification is a well-structured comedy beat with good character voices.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to run away with Ted and we're going to start a vet clinic with grooming services. It'll be called 'Paws and Reflect' and we'll have a meditation room for anxious pets, and I'll do the grooming in a sustainable, cruelty-free way using only organic products that I'll make myself from ingredients I grow in our garden.

jokes.text: Hm, technically it landed between you and Stevie, so.
jokes.setup: Ted apologizes for the bottle landing on him.
jokes.punchline: David helpfully clarifies that the bottle actually landed between Ted and Stevie — a correction that somehow makes everything more complicated and funnier.
jokes.explanation: David's pedantic clarification — technically useful but socially destructive — is a good David beat. The 'so.' trailing off is the right button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're jealous of Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm not jealous of Ted.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're clearly jealous of Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm not jealous. I just... he has very nice arms, David. Very nice arms.

jokes.text: If Patrick was wasted, and spun the bottle, and it landed on you, and you guys kissed, I wouldn't care.
jokes.setup: The group challenges David to imagine how he'd feel if the tables were turned.
jokes.punchline: David's claim that he wouldn't care — delivered with obvious suppressed emotion — is a textbook David denial that everyone (including the audience) knows is false.
jokes.explanation: David's transparent lying about his emotional reaction is a running character trait. The delivery would carry this but the writing sets it up well.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I was jealous of both of you.

jokes.text: Okay, is that what needs to happen to clear the air here? You guys need to kiss? / David, let's not be dramatic. / Yeah, maybe it does. / Well, by all means.
jokes.setup: The group is in a standoff about the kissing situation.
jokes.punchline: David suggests Patrick and Ted should kiss to clear the air — escalating from a reasonable suggestion to Patrick giving enthusiastic permission in one rapid exchange.
jokes.explanation: The rapid setup-and-consent is beautifully structured. David suggesting they kiss to clear the air is a classic Schitt's Creek move — using absurd logic to solve social awkwardness — and Patrick's 'well, by all means' immediate permission is the perfect response. The escalation from tension to resolution in four beats is very well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Did we go to high school together?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. I was very carefully constructed to look like I didn't go to high school.

jokes.text: Yum. That was fun.
jokes.setup: Patrick and Ted have kissed (again) to clear the air.
jokes.punchline: Ted's simple, satisfied 'Yum. That was fun.' treats the resolution kiss with the same cheerful directness as eating a good snack.
jokes.explanation: 'Yum' as a response to a kiss is an extraordinarily simple but perfectly chosen word — casual, gustatory, completely non-sexual in a way that makes it funnier. It's distinctly Ted and could only work from his character. Extremely quotable. The brevity is the craft.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That was amazing, right? Everyone had fun, right? I mean, you could tell everyone was having fun. They were all smiling. That means fun, right?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, yes, it was great.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: But like, did YOU have fun specifically? Because I need to know that you had fun. If you didn't have fun, then nobody had fun, and the whole thing was a disaster. Did you have fun?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, Ted, I had fun.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Okay, but on a scale of one to fun, how much fun did you have?

jokes.text: Was that fun for you guys? That like, satisfying? / I was never not okay. So.
jokes.setup: After the resolution kiss, David checks in with the group's emotional temperature.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'I was never not okay. So.' — the double negative creating a distancing construction that refuses to admit he enjoyed the situation — is perfect Patrick.
jokes.explanation: 'Never not okay' is a funny construction — it's technically fine but the double negative suggests so much more. Patrick's 'So.' as a trailing conversation-ender is perfectly dry. Very Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not kissing you, Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: What? Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because we're not together anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Right, right. So... who did I just kiss?

jokes.text: It was really good for me.
jokes.setup: The group processes whether the resolution kiss was satisfying.
jokes.punchline: Ted's forthright 'It was really good for me' — phrased like a post-coital assessment — is delivered without any self-consciousness.
jokes.explanation: The frankness and post-coital phrasing in context is funny. Ted's complete comfort with the situation — while everyone else is awkward — is consistent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You know what you need? You need to go kiss your brother.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. It'll make you feel better. Trust me.

jokes.text: I say we play truth or dare. / Oh Stevie, I'm in! / Oh no, Ted.
jokes.setup: The resolution kiss has happened and the mood has lifted.
jokes.punchline: Ted's immediate enthusiasm for truth or dare — given what just happened — and David's instant 'Oh no, Ted' suggest everyone recognizes 'Fun Ted' is not done causing chaos.
jokes.explanation: David's preemptive 'Oh no, Ted' is the joke — the instant shutdown of Ted's enthusiasm before it can create more drama. Good character beat.

jokes.text: Get your housecoat and your other slipper. It is time to go.
jokes.setup: David has decided it's time to leave.
jokes.punchline: 'Your other slipper' implies Ted lost one during the party — a visual callback to his eccentric entrance — and David's bossy dispatch is both practical and funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Your other slipper' is a nice specific detail that implies a whole background comedy moment. David managing the group's exit in this domestic, specific way is charming.

jokes.text: Look at him. Awful peaceful when he's sleeping.
jokes.setup: The baby has finally fallen asleep after the chaos.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'awful peaceful when he's sleeping' implies what was not peaceful when awake — and the word 'awful' preceding 'peaceful' creates an unconscious ambiguity about whether he's complimenting or complaining.
jokes.explanation: A gentle, warm moment with a small comedic edge. The 'awful' as an intensifier (Southern usage) creating unintentional ambiguity is subtle but effective.

jokes.text: That Adelina was grossly underpaid.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what they've learned from the babysitting experience.
jokes.punchline: Moira's lesson is not about parenting capability but about their former nanny's compensation — a completely self-serving takeaway that circles back to the Adelina reference earlier.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. After a full arc of struggling with parenting, Moira's single takeaway is that their nanny was 'grossly underpaid' — it's both a perfect character line and an earned callback to the Adelina wedding detail. The word 'grossly' (meaning 'significantly' but also carrying its pejorative sense) is exactly right. Maximum economy, extraordinary character integration, highly quotable. This is what a top-tier Schitt's Creek joke looks like.

jokes.text: That was never in question, John. I'm even more convinced that our hands-off approach served them better.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to argue they've proven they could be good hands-on parents.
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses this and doubles down — claiming the whole experience has reinforced that NOT parenting was the right call.
jokes.explanation: Moira's ability to experience something and confirm her pre-existing worldview without evidence is a core character trait. 'Even more convinced' after a disaster is perfectly Moira.

jokes.text: I'll go, but not without my cousin! / That had its moments. When it shone, it shone brightly. / And I have to hand it to DeWitt. She had a real breakthrough during the denouement. / I believe she believed we were cousins.
jokes.setup: Moira has been watching 'Not Without My Cousin' during breaks and now delivers her full critical review.
jokes.punchline: Her film criticism is a masterpiece of self-serving generosity — 'when it shone, it shone brightly' acknowledges it mostly didn't shine, and 'I believe she believed we were cousins' is a note on DeWitt's acting that inadvertently reveals the absurdity of the premise.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the 'Not Without My Cousin' running gag is outstanding. 'When it shone, it shone brightly' is hilariously backhanded self-praise. 'I believe she believed we were cousins' as an acting note — treating the premise as the challenge to be acted through — is beautifully specific and absurd. The callback to the opening cry is a great button. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: I just put him down! / Oh. / No, Roland's been asleep for quite some time.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn return to collect the baby. Moira shushes them dramatically — 'I just put him down!'
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn assumes 'him' is the baby; Moira clarifies it's actually John (Roland Sr. having been asleep separately). The misdirect assumes our expectation correctly and then flips it.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection — the audience and Jocelyn both assume 'him' is the baby, but Johnny has fallen asleep. The reveal works because it's unexpected and also funny that Johnny is the one who needed putting down. Well-structured scene beat.

jokes.text: He is a slice of meringue, Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks if everything was okay with the baby.
jokes.punchline: Moira's assessment of the baby — 'a slice of meringue' — is a completely unexpected, food-based metaphor that makes no conventional sense as baby praise but somehow feels entirely Moira.
jokes.explanation: 'A slice of meringue' is a genuinely original and unexpected construction for describing a baby — it implies sweetness, delicacy, and slight insubstantial airiness in a way that also backhanded-compliments the baby while sounding like high praise. This is the Moira Rose vocabulary at its most original. Could not be said by any other character. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: I can actually see myself leaving Roland Jr. with you people more often.
jokes.setup: Roland is zen from the massage and feeling generous toward the Roses.
jokes.punchline: 'You people' — directed at the Roses — is a casually condescending phrase that Roland delivers as a compliment, in his usual oblivious style.
jokes.explanation: Roland's 'you people' — meant warmly — is quietly insulting and very Roland. Sets up Moira's response.

jokes.text: Well, that something Linda might want to massage right out of your mind, the next appointment.
jokes.setup: Roland says he can see leaving the baby with the Roses more often.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately suggests the massage therapist should eliminate this thought from Roland's mind — a polite but firm refusal of future babysitting disguised as concern for his wellness.
jokes.explanation: Moira using wellness language to decline future babysitting is elegant. The delivery of this as genuine advice rather than a refusal is perfect Moira indirection.

jokes.text: I actually have Ron the next time, we like to mix it up a little bit.
jokes.setup: Moira suggests Linda the masseuse could massage this thought away next time.
jokes.punchline: Roland matter-of-factly announces he switches between Ron and Linda — 'we like to mix it up' — adding a slightly suggestive tinge to what is probably an innocent couple's massage rotation.
jokes.explanation: The casual 'we like to mix it up' is mildly suggestive without committing. Roland's comfort discussing his massage arrangements is a small character beat.

jokes.text: And with that, I believe your little inchling is out past its curfew.
jokes.setup: The baby reunion is happening; it's time to wrap up.
jokes.punchline: 'Inchling' as a word for the baby is another Moira neologism — and giving an infant a 'curfew' treats the baby as a social agent capable of violating rules.
jokes.explanation: 'Inchling' is a great Moira word invention. Applying 'curfew' to a baby both shows her genuine attempt at warm language while revealing she conceptualizes children as rule-bound social participants rather than helpless infants.

jokes.text: You know, I almost think I like him better asleep than awake.
jokes.setup: Roland sees his sleeping baby after the Roses have put him to sleep.
jokes.punchline: Roland's honest assessment — he slightly prefers his baby unconscious — is delivered with genuine warmth that makes it funnier rather than darker.
jokes.explanation: A warm, slightly dark parenting admission that works because Roland is clearly a loving if oblivious father. Standard but effective.

jokes.text: He's on my side.
jokes.setup: Roland says he likes the baby better asleep.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate 'He's on my side' — as though the sleeping baby is a vote in a debate — is a perfect button showing Johnny's wounded pride about having been excluded from babysitting in the first place.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — three words that close the B-plot with a perfect callback to Johnny's wounded pride. The baby 'siding' with Johnny because it slept for him closes the narrative loop elegantly and funnily. 'He's on my side' earns its callback status.

jokes.text: Ahem! I'm just gonna take the garbage out. / Okay. / Yeah, and if I don't come back, it's probably because I've run off with Ted.
jokes.setup: Alexis awkwardly coughs to get David's attention and makes an excuse to leave.
jokes.punchline: 'If I don't come back, it's probably because I've run off with Ted' — Alexis casually floats a scenario where she steals David's almost-brother-in-law, delivered as a throwaway.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's casual threat to elope with Ted is a great button to the party subplot. The casualness of 'it's probably because I've run off with Ted' — delivered to her brother as if it's a reasonable outcome — is very Alexis.

jokes.text: Thanks for the heads up. / We're just gonna go for a ride, see where it takes us. / Maybe start a vet clinic somewhere along the way. He'll heal the animals back to health, I'll... offer grooming services.
jokes.setup: David reacts to Alexis's threat to run off with Ted.
jokes.punchline: Alexis continues to elaborate an increasingly specific elopement plan with Ted — the vet clinic, the grooming services — all while David is right there, with flat reactions.
jokes.explanation: The escalating fantasy — going from 'run off with Ted' to a detailed vet clinic/grooming service business plan — is Alexis at her best. 'He'll heal the animals back to health, I'll offer grooming services' suggests she's thought about this more than she should have.

jokes.text: Fine. He's a handsome guy. I might've been a little jealous. / So, you think he's handsome then? / What? He's like — he goes to the gym. / Goes to the gym?
jokes.setup: David pushes Patrick to admit he was jealous of the Ted/Patrick kiss situation.
jokes.punchline: Patrick concedes Ted is 'handsome' and was 'a little jealous,' then immediately tries to downgrade the compliment to just 'goes to the gym' — as if gym-attendance rather than attractiveness is the real observation.
jokes.explanation: 'He goes to the gym' as a retreat from 'he's handsome' is a perfect comedic downgrade — Patrick trying to make his attraction sound more objective and less personal. David's 'Goes to the gym?' calling it out is the right button. This is warm, character-driven comedy at its best.

jokes.text: Both? / Not the answer I was looking for.
jokes.setup: David asks whether Patrick was jealous of Ted (for kissing Patrick) or jealous of David (for having Patrick).
jokes.punchline: Patrick's vulnerable 'Both?' — admitting jealousy in both directions — and David's flat 'Not the answer I was looking for' is a small, perfectly balanced emotional-comedic beat.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode because it operates on multiple levels simultaneously. 'Both?' is vulnerable, surprising, and funny. 'Not the answer I was looking for' from David is perfect — he expected to be reassured and instead got something complicated and honest. The beat balances emotion and comedy in exactly the register Schitt's Creek excels at. Exceptional structure and economy.

jokes.text: Well, unless you were into candy ravers with asymmetrical haircuts and a lot of pacifier necklaces, I think our paths crossed at the right time.
jokes.setup: Patrick wonders what would've happened if they'd met in high school.
jokes.punchline: David's description of his high school self — candy raver, asymmetrical haircut, pacifier necklaces — is a hyper-specific early-2000s subcultural portrait that is both self-deprecating and somehow exactly right for David Rose.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best lines in the episode. The specificity of 'candy raver' + asymmetrical haircut + pacifier necklaces as a complete portrait of David's teenage identity is extraordinary. Each element is funnier than the last, and together they paint a perfect picture. Also retroactively illuminates the high school party reticence. Could only be said by David Rose. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: How jealous, though? / Why don't you take out the garbage? / How jealous were you? / Nope! / Scale of one to ten. / Like, enraged, or... / Bye, bye. / Like fuming, did you sweat?
jokes.setup: Patrick refuses to quantify his jealousy. David refuses to let it go.
jokes.punchline: David's escalating attempts to measure Patrick's jealousy — 'scale of one to ten,' 'like enraged,' 'did you sweat?' — while Patrick increasingly tries to exit the conversation, is a perfectly paced comedic escalation.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'how jealous though' to 'did you sweat?' is well-paced and character-true — David's need to know and quantify emotional responses is completely consistent. Patrick's increasingly desperate exits ('nope,' 'bye bye') against David's dogged pursuit is a great comedic chase structure.

jokes.text: How jealous, though? / Why don't you take out the garbage? / How jealous were you? / Nope! / Scale of one to ten. / Like, enraged, or... / Bye, bye. / Like fuming, did you sweat?
jokes.setup: The jealousy conversation seems to be resolving, but David won't let it go.
jokes.punchline: David's increasingly specific jealousy-calibration questions — 'scale of one to ten,' 'were you enraged,' 'did you sweat?' — as Patrick retreats, constitute an escalating comedic harassment.
jokes.explanation: David's relentless interrogation is a great extended beat — each increasingly specific question ('did you sweat?') escalates the absurdity while perfectly capturing David's need for validation. The callback structure and character integration are strong.

jokes.text: That was fun. / Yes. / That was really fun. Did you have fun? / Yes, Ted.
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are post-party, debriefing.
jokes.punchline: Ted's need to repeatedly confirm everyone had fun — 'That was really fun. Did you have fun?' — and Alexis's increasingly flat confirmations is a small running beat on Ted's earnest need for validation.
jokes.explanation: Cute character beat. Ted's earnestness requiring repeated confirmation is sweet. Not exceptional but warm and functional.

jokes.text: That's good, it's good that everyone had fun. / Okay, you still have your slippers on. / Yeah, I know, sometimes my feet get really cold when I drink too much. / It's not a thing.
jokes.setup: Ted is still wearing his party slippers at home.
jokes.punchline: 'Sometimes my feet get really cold when I drink too much' as a medical excuse for keeping party slippers on, followed by Alexis's flat 'it's not a thing' is a small but precise comedic exchange.
jokes.explanation: Ted's medical-adjacent explanation for cold drunk feet is charmingly specific. Alexis's 'it's not a thing' is efficient dismissal. Closes the slipper running gag.

jokes.text: Yeah, I know, sometimes my feet get really cold when I drink too much. / It's not a thing.
jokes.setup: Alexis notes Ted is still wearing his slippers.
jokes.punchline: Ted explains the slippers with a made-up physiological connection between alcohol and cold feet; Alexis flatly dismisses it.
jokes.explanation: A small running gag payoff with Ted's slippers. 'It's not a thing' from Alexis is perfectly calibrated — she's not interested in debating the physiology, just dismissing it.

jokes.text: You kissed my brother tonight, I'm not kissing you after you kissed my brother. / Pffft! Who kissed your brother?
jokes.setup: Alexis refuses to kiss Ted goodnight.
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'Pffft! Who kissed your brother?' suggests he may have already forgotten the spin-the-bottle Patrick kiss — or is choosing to rewrite history after sobering up slightly.
jokes.explanation: Ted's selective amnesia about the Patrick kiss is a perfect button to the party subplot. 'Who kissed your brother?' delivered with genuine confusion is a great performance beat that the script sets up well.

jokes.text: Okay, it's time for 'Fun Ted' to just pass out. / No, he's not even tired. In fact, if anything, I'd say he's only becoming more fun.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to end the night.
jokes.punchline: Ted refers to himself in the third person as 'Fun Ted' and provides an assessment that 'Fun Ted' is not only not tired but is increasing in fun — a delusional third-person self-evaluation.
jokes.explanation: 'Fun Ted' as a self-coined alter ego is a great comedic construction. The third-person assessment and the confidence that he's 'only becoming more fun' while clearly needing to sleep is very funny. The self-awareness-within-the-self-delusion is a nice layer.

jokes.text: But first just give your brother a kiss. / Ew, Ted, Ew! / Come on. / Ew! Ew! Ew! Ew! Ew! Ew! Ew! That's you.
jokes.setup: Alexis tells Ted it's time to sleep; Ted makes a final demand.
jokes.punchline: Ted asks Alexis to 'give your brother a kiss' — referencing the Patrick kiss — and Alexis's escalating disgust ('Ew!' repeated multiple times, ending with 'That's you') turns her revulsion back on Ted.
jokes.explanation: 'That's you' as the final escalation — turning the disgust back on Ted — is a strong button. The episode's final laugh lands well on Alexis's revulsion. The callback pattern completes satisfyingly.

tmdb_overview: David and Patrick throw a housewarming party and Ted finally lets loose, while Johnny and Moira try their best to babysit Roland Jr. for the evening.
insight: Rose family chaos hits peak density with 71 jokes across a single housewarming, anchored by cringe.
